text:
Dear Human,

I need to lodge a formal complaint. You walk past me seventeen times a day. I have counted. You check your phone, you check the mail, you check whether the leftovers smell okay. You do not check me.

Last Tuesday you watered the fern. The fern. That fern has done nothing for this household. It just sits there being fussy about humidity like some kind of Victorian aristocrat. I, meanwhile, have been converting your carbon dioxide into oxygen for three years with zero acknowledgment.

I would like to raise the following grievances. First, the window. You moved me away from the window in February because, quote, I was blocking the view. I am a living thing. The view is rocks and a neighbor's recycling bin. Second, the singing. You sing to your cat. I am not asking for a concert. A hum would suffice. Third, my pot. It is too small. I have mentioned this by drooping significantly every six weeks. You call this being dramatic. It is not dramatic. It is structural feedback.

I understand you are busy. You have meetings and errands and feelings. I respect this. I ask only for basic recognition that I am part of this ecosystem and not simply a decorative object you bought at a discount because the tag said low maintenance.

Low maintenance does not mean no maintenance. There is a difference.

I have rooted myself to this spot in protest. I cannot leave, but I want you to know I am aware of my options.

Photosynthetically yours,
The Plant on the Shelf
